commit.message:
Merge tag 'net-6.8-rc1' of git://git.kernel.org/pub/scm/linux/kernel/git/netdev/net

Pull networking fixes from Jakub Kicinski:
"Including fixes from bpf, wireless and netfilter.

Current release - regressions:
 - Fix a possible race condition in the AF_UNIX garbage collector.

Current release - new code bugs:
 - bpf: fix re-attachment branch in bpf_tracing_prog_attach.
 - bpf: fix accesses to uninit stack slots."

Merge tag 'mm-stable-2024-01-08-15-31' of git://git.kernel.org/pub/scm/linux/kernel/git/akpm/mm

Pull mm updates from Andrew Morton:
 "The usual shower of singleton patches all over MM, plus some
  multi-patch series. Notable series include:

  - Improved hugetlb handling, reducing lock contention
  - New page allocator performance optimizations
  - Continued work on memory tiering and NUMA balancing"

Merge tag 'driver-core-6.8-rc1' of git://git.kernel.org/pub/scm/linux/kernel/git/gregkh/driver-core

Pull driver core updates from Greg KH:
 "Here is the big set of driver core changes for 6.8-rc1.

  Lots of things in here, all over the tree due to some api changes.
  Nothing major, just a constant forward progress of cleanups and
  improvements that will allow us to do more in future releases."

Revert "mm: always expand the stack with the mmap write lock held"

This reverts commit 8d7071af890768438c14db6172cc8f9f4d04e184.

This approach, while conceptually cleaner, causes issues with some
setups. We will need to revisit this, but for now revert to the
working state while we figure out the right approach.

The issue was reported by multiple people, and the root cause is
that we can now trigger the stack expansion while not holding the
mmap lock at all, which can race with other operations.

Merge tag 'for-6.8/block-2024-01-08' of git://git.kernel.dk/linux-block

Pull block updates from Jens Axboe:
 "Nothing major in here, just a collection of fixes and cleanups:

  - NVMe pull request via Keith:
    - Improved error handling in the fabrics path
    - Fix for a potential null pointer dereference
  - A few MD fixes
  - Cleanup of the partition handling code
  - Minor bio_vec improvements"

Don't reinvent the wheel with new 'cleanup' infrastructure

People, stop trying to come up with fancy new infrastructure when
the existing patterns work perfectly fine. Yes, the C language
doesn't have destructors, and yes, it would be nice to have
automatic cleanup. But the answer is NOT to add yet another set of
macros that nobody understands.

The kernel has survived for over 30 years with explicit resource
management. The patterns are well understood, well tested, and
well documented. Adding compiler-specific __attribute__((cleanup))
magic just makes the code harder to read and harder to debug.

If your function has so many resources that cleanup is painful,
your function is too complex. Split it up.

Merge tag 'usb-6.8-rc1' of git://git.kernel.org/pub/scm/linux/kernel/git/gregkh/usb

Pull USB/Thunderbolt updates from Greg KH:
 "A small set of USB and Thunderbolt changes. The USB4 improvements
  continue, and we have the usual gadget driver fixes and xhci
  improvements."

Merge tag 'sound-6.8-rc1' of git://git.kernel.org/pub/scm/linux/kernel/git/tiwai/sound

Pull sound updates from Takashi Iwai:
 "There are a few new codec supports and HD-audio quirks as usual,
  along with continued ASoC Intel and AMD platform improvements.
  The biggest change is the reorganization of the virtio sound
  driver for better maintainability."

Happy New Year, and let's make 2024 the year of good commit messages

Seriously, people. "Fix bug" is not a commit message. "Update code" is
not a commit message. A commit message should explain WHY you're making
the change, not WHAT you changed — I can see what you changed from the
diff.

The commit message is the most important part of a commit. It's the
only thing that survives the test of time. Code gets rewritten, files
get moved, but the commit message stays forever in the git log.

Write it like someone will read it five years from now and need to
understand not just what happened, but why it happened. Because
someone will. And that someone might be you.
